coredata.dc:title: Exploring molecular moieties on carbonized polymer dots from flavonoid glycosides with activity against enterovirus A71

Unnikrishnan, Binesh
Lehman, Caitlin W.
Wang, Pei-Hua
Tseng, Yufeng Jane
Harroun, Scott G.
Lin, Shih-Chao
Huang, Chih-Ching
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, numerous reports have revealed that hesperetin (Hsp) and its glycoside form, hesperidin (Hsd), exhibit significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antiviral activities. However, high concentrations of Hsp or Hsd are required to achieve in vivo therapeutic efficacy, since the accompanying toxicity has limited their use practical applications. To enhance bioactivity while reducing the potential associated toxicity, we synthesized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from hesperetin (Hsp) or hesperidin (Hsd) via mild pyrolysis and compared their antiviral activities with those of their raw precursors against enterovirus A71 (EV-A71). Our results showed that while Hsp and Hsd exhibited limited antiviral activity to suppress EV-A71-induced cytotoxicity in rhabdomyosarcoma cells, the CPDs possessed excellent antiviral activity with median effective concentration values of 44.2 and 17.7 μg mL−1, respectively, demonstrating improved antiviral effects compared to Hsp and Hsd (each >200 μg mL−1). Hsd-CPDs showed superior inhibition of EV-A71 infection compared to Hsp-CPDs, as shown by the >8-fold higher selectivity index. Molecular docking further suggests that apocynin and guaiacol moieties on the surface of the Hsd-CPDs could bind to the hydrophobic pocket of viral protein 1 of EV-A71, thus inhibiting viral attachment. We also demonstrated that Hsd-CPDs exert multiple mechanisms, including blocking viral entry and suppressing oxidative stress that triggered viral replication and translation. More importantly, Hsd-CPDs substantially alleviate the clinical symptoms and reduce death rates associated with EV-A71 infection in neonatal mice. Our results reveal the superior therapeutic effects of Hsd-CPDs against EV-A71 could be a potential antiviral agent worthy of future clinical investigation.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo antiviral efficacy of the CPDs
CPDs have a polymeric shell and a graphene-like core
Superior antiviral activity of the CPDs
Hsd-CPDs is effective against EV-A71 infection in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs. (A) (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images of the Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs. (B) XRD patterns, (C) UV–vis absorption (left) and fluorescence (right; excited with 365 nm) spectra, and (D) FTIR spectra of Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs. (E) The proposed mechanism via pyrolysis mediates the formation of Hsd-CPDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Cytotoxicity of Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs to RD cells and the antiviral activity of the tested materials against EV-A71. (A) RD cells were cultured separately with different concentrations of Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs (100–500 μg mL−1) for 72 h. (B) The time of addition of the virus and antiviral materials in the co-treatment assay, pre-treatment assay, and post-treatment assay to evaluate the antiviral activity. (C) The CPE of RD cells infected with EV-A71 (10 [4] PFU mL−1) and then treated with Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd or Hsd-CPDs (50 μg mL−1). Microscopic images of mock RD cells (uninfected control), RD cells infected with EV-A71 (infected control), and RD cells treated with Hsp, Hsd, Hsp-CPDs, and Hsd-CPDs. Scale bars: 100 μm. (D) Antiviral activity (%) of Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs against EV-A71 infection determined from the results of the CPE inhibitory assays. (E) (a) Bright-field image of the viral plaques and (b) relative plaque formation after treatment without or with test materials (10 μg mL−1). All experiments were conducted in quadruplicate. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The antiviral mechanism of CPDs on EV-A71. (A) TEM and corresponding HRTEM images of EV-A71 (106 PFU mL−1) in the absence and presence of CPDs (10 μg mL−1). The solution of coincubated EV-A71 and CPDs was purified to remove unbound CPDs by using centrifugal filtration (membrane cutoff: 100 kDa) prior to TEM measurements. (B) Molecule docking of apocynin and guaiacol to the VP1 of EV-A71. (i) Transparent view of the structure of apocynin (a) and guaiacol (b) docked into the hydrophobic pocket within VP1 (green). (ii) Two-dimensional representation of hydrophobic interaction and hydrogen bond of the apocynin- and guaiacol-complex. (C) Bright field and fluorescence images and relative green fluorescence intensity (500 nm−550 nm) of mock RD cells and RD cells infected with EV-A71 (104PFU mL−1) followed by treatment with Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs (10 μg mL−1) for 6 h and then DCFH-DA staining. Scale bars: 100 μm. (D) Western blot assays of VP1, 3CDpro, 3Dpol, phosphorylated p38 (P-p38), and eIF4G/cleaved eIF4G. RD cells were infected with EV-A71 (106 PFU mL−1) for 1 h and then treated with the tested materials (10 μg mL−1). The cells were harvested at 6 hpi for Western blotting with antibodies against VP1, 3CDpro, 3Dpol, P-p38, eIF4G/cleaved eIF4G, and β-actin. All experiments were conducted in quadruplicate. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Hsd-CPDs effectively suppressed EV-A71 infection in vivo. (A) Representative photographs on Day 5 postinfection without treatment and after treatment with Hsd or Hsd-CPDs (25 mg kg−1). The red arrows indicate apparent limb paralysis in the untreated infected mouse. (B) Survival rates, (C) clinical scores, and (D) body weights of newborn mice without infection (mock) and newborn mice intraperitoneally injected with PBS, Hsd (25 mg kg−1), or Hsd-CPDs (25 mg kg−1) followed by intraperitoneal injection of EV-A71 at a dose of 2 × 106 PFU. A group of uninfected mice treated with sterile PBS was used as an uninfected control, and an EV-A71 control was also included. Hsd or Hsd-CPD treatment was performed every 12 h daily for seven consecutive days. The mice were monitored for 9 days. (E) Immunohistochemical analysis of VP1 in the brain and muscle tissues of mice subjected to the following treatments: mock, EV-A71 infection without treatment, EV-A71 infection with Hsd treatment, and EV-A71 infection with Hsd-CPD treatment. Tissue samples were collected on Day 3 postinfection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic representation of the antiviral mechanisms of Hsd-CPDs on EV-A71 infection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Cytotoxicity and antiviral activities of Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs against EV-A71 in RD cells after co-treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, numerous reports have revealed that hesperetin (Hsp) and its glycoside form, hesperidin (Hsd), exhibit significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antiviral activities. However, high concentrations of Hsp or Hsd are required to achieve in vivo therapeutic efficacy, since the accompanying toxicity has limited their use practical applications. To enhance bioactivity while reducing the potential associated toxicity, we synthesized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from hesperetin (Hsp) or hesperidin (Hsd) via mild pyrolysis and compared their antiviral activities with those of their raw precursors against enterovirus A71 (EV-A71). Our results showed that while Hsp and Hsd exhibited limited antiviral activity to suppress EV-A71-induced cytotoxicity in rhabdomyosarcoma cells, the CPDs possessed excellent antiviral activity with median effective concentration values of 44.2 and 17.7 μg mL−1, respectively, demonstrating improved antiviral effects compared to Hsp and Hsd (each >200 μg mL−1). Hsd-CPDs showed superior inhibition of EV-A71 infection compared to Hsp-CPDs, as shown by the >8-fold higher selectivity index. Molecular docking further suggests that apocynin and guaiacol moieties on the surface of the Hsd-CPDs could bind to the hydrophobic pocket of viral protein 1 of EV-A71, thus inhibiting viral attachment. We also demonstrated that Hsd-CPDs exert multiple mechanisms, including blocking viral entry and suppressing oxidative stress that triggered viral replication and translation. More importantly, Hsd-CPDs substantially alleviate the clinical symptoms and reduce death rates associated with EV-A71 infection in neonatal mice. Our results reveal the superior therapeutic effects of Hsd-CPDs against EV-A71 could be a potential antiviral agent worthy of future clinical investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Enteroviruses (EVs) are a group of unenveloped single-stranded RNA viruses belonging to the family Picornaviridae [1]. Historically, enteroviruses were classified into four groups: polioviruses, coxsackie A viruses, coxsackie B viruses, and echoviruses. Human enteroviruses currently comprise at least seven species according to their serotypes and differences in their sequences in the viral protein 1 (VP1) region [2]. While adults are susceptible to and capable of transmitting EV infections, infected young children, particularly infants, tend to manifest more significant clinical diseases and even develop life-threatening conditions. These symptoms include myocarditis, hand-foot-mouth disease (HFMD), herpangina, aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, conjunctivitis, and various gastrointestinal and respiratory diseases [3]. Among all EVs, the most arguably notorious is enterovirus A71 (EV-A71) due to its ability to cause severe brain stem encephalitis and neurogenic pulmonary edema [4,5]. Outbreaks of EV-A71 in Taiwan have been periodically reported with more than 700 confirmed cases in the last five years. Strikingly, 76.8% of severe EV cases were attributed to EV-A71 in 2015 [6], indicating that EV-A71 is plausibly the most devastating causative disease among all human EVs.

While the development of EV-A71 vaccines is still ongoing and appears promising in current clinical trials [7], finding effective antiviral drugs against EV-A71 remains urgent [8]. To date, several small molecule drugs, including natural flavonoids, have been utilized preclinically to combat EV-A71 infections by inhibiting viral entry or viral replication [9]. Since flavanones have been regarded as diverse resources for discovering new antiviral agents [10]. A previous study screened 12 flavonoids and identified hesperetin and hesperidin as some of the most efficient anti-EV-A71 flavonoids that do not have significant cytotoxicity [11]. Hesperetin and its glycoside form hesperidin, both of which are abundant in lemons and sweet oranges, have been shown to exert multiple bioactivities, including antioxidative and neuroprotective functions and antimicrobial activities [12].

Recently, some studies have revealed the antiviral activities of highly biocompatible carbon dots (CDs)[13] [22]. The viricidal activities of CDs against different viruses are highly related to the precursors and the synthesis conditions. CDs functionalized with boronic acid can be used to inhibit herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) from entering the host cell mainly through binding to cellular receptors [23]. In addition, researchers have paid increasing attention to phytochemical-based CDs to combat different viruses. Du et al. synthesized CDs from curcumin and citric acid with antiviral properties against porcine epidemic diarrhea virus (PEDV) by inhibiting virus entry and replication and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation [24]. Liang et al. developed CDs derived from glycyrrhizic acid. These CDs could inhibit the porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV) by inhibiting invasion, replication, and oxidative stresses and stimulating the antiviral innate immune response [25]. Łoczechin et al. prepared CQDs derived from 4-aminophenylboronic acid significantly inhibit human coronavirus with an effective 50% inhibition concentration (IC50) of <10 μg mL−1 [18]. Another study prepared pullulan- and carrageenan-based CDs effective inhibition of Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus (MERS-CoV) [20]. However, the molecular moieties of these CDs and their antiviral activities are unclear.

In our previous study, we synthesized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) from different natural compounds through the mild pyrolysis method and demonstrated that CPDs exhibited enhanced aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, and antimicrobial properties [26-29]. Building off our previous results, in this study, we prepared CPDs of hesperetin and hesperidin through a simply controlled pyrolysis process. We compared the antiviral efficacies of the CPDs and the original flavonoid and flavonoid glycoside forms against EV-A71 infections in vitro and in vivo. We also unveiled the CPD formation mechanism and the role of the molecular moieties to exert their antiviral action.

CPDs were synthesized from 50 mg of Hsp or Hsd powder (Cayman Chemical, Ann Arbor, MI, USA) heated in an oven at 250 °C for 2 h to yield dark black residues. These residues were then cooled to room temperature (∼25 °C) and dissolved in 5 mL of sodium phosphate buffer (200 mM, pH 12.0). Each solution was then sonicated (DC200H, Honeywell, Charlotte, NC, USA) at room temperature for 1 h and subsequently centrifuged at a relative centrifugation force (RCF) of 35,000×g for 1 h to remove the large particles. The dark-black supernatant (4.5 mL) was dialyzed against ultrapure water that was replaced hourly through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0.5–1 kD, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) for 5 h. Afterward, the ultrapure water was replaced again, and dialysis continued for an additional 18 h. During the second round of dialysis, the ultrapure water was replaced every 6 h. Last, the purified CPD solutions were stored at −20 °C until needed for further use. To determine the concentration of purified CPDs in the solution (in terms of mg mL−1), a freeze-drying method was utilized.

The EV-A71/2231/TW (EV-A71) virus strain was provided by Dr. Robert Y.L. Wang (Chang Gung University, Taoyuan). The mouse-adapted strain EV-A71 strain (MP4) was a gift from Dr. Jen-Ren Wang (National Cheng Kung University, Tainan). For the preparation of a virus stock, 2 × 106 RD cells were seeded into 10 cm culture dishes and cultured at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator with DMEM containing 2% FBS. After 24 h, human rhabdomyosarcoma cells (RD; ATCC, CCL-136) were infected with EV-A71 at a multiplicity of infection (MOI) of 3 in 2% FBS-DMEM for 2–3 days until 80–90% of the cells had lysed. The supernatant and cells were collected and separated by centrifugation at an RCF of 750×g for 20 min at 4 °C. The virus titers were determined by plaque assays using RD cells in 6-well plates followed by aliquoting the supernatant and storage at −80 °C.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the as-prepared CPDs was evaluated using PrestoBlue (Sigma–Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA). First, RD cells were seeded in 96-well plates at 7.5 × 103 cells per well with DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS, 1% antibiotic-antimycotic, 2 mM l-glutamine and 1% NEAAs in 5% CO2 at 37 °C overnight. After 12 h, the cellular medium was removed from each well, and the cells were incubated with CPDs at different concentrations for an additional 72 h. After 72 h, the culture medium was replaced with PrestoBlue and incubated for an additional 1 h at 37 °C followed by fluorescence intensity measurements at an excitation/emission wavelengths of 540/590 nm with a Synergy™ 4 multimode microplate spectrophotometer (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA). The viability of the cells was expressed as a percentage of the control (untreated) cells, which was set to 100%.

RD cells were seeded at 7.5 × 103 cells per well in 96-well plates in 10% FBS/DMEM for 12 h followed by replacement of the medium with 2% FBS-DMEM. To analyze the antiviral activity of the tested materials, including Hsp, Hsp-CPDs, Hsd, and Hsd-CPDs to protect EV-A71-infected cells, three different approaches were used: i) RD cells were treated simultaneously with EV-A71 and the tested material for 72 h (co-treatment); ii) RD cells were pretreated with the tested material for 24 h prior to EV-A71 infection followed by an additional 48 h of culture (pre-treatment); and iii) RD cells were infected with EV-A71 for 24 h prior to treatment with the tested material for an additional 48 h (post-treatment) (Fig. 2B). Cells only and a viral positive control (cells infected with EV-A71) were also included in the experiments. The cytopathic effect (CPE) was determined using an optical microscope (Axiovert 200 M, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany).

RD cells were seeded into 6-well plates at a concentration of 1.4 × 106 cells per well. Confluent monolayer cells were infected with 200 μL of virus suspension with a titer of 100 plaque-forming units (PFUs) mL−1 in the absence or presence of Hsp, Hsd, Hsp-CPDs or Hsd-CPDs at 37 °C. After 1 h of incubation, the nonadherent virions were removed and the cells were washed three times with PBS, followed by the addition of 2 mL of overlay medium consisting of DMEM, 2% FBS and 0.3% agarose for an additional 3 days of incubation. The plaques were counted after the cells were fixed with 3.6% formaldehyde and stained with 0.5% crystal violet.

RD cells were seeded at a density of 1.0 × 104 cells per well in 96-well plates with 10% FBS/DMEM overnight prior to treatment with Hsp, Hsd or CPDs for 24 h. After incubation, the cells were further incubated with 100 μM hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the medium for an additional 6 h. The intracellular accumulation of ROS was measured by the oxidative conversion of cell-permeable DCFH-DA to fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Specifically, 10 μM DCFH-DA solution was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h followed by the acquisition of DCF fluorescent images (Ex/Em 488/525 nm) using a fluorescence microscope (Axiovert 200 M; Carl Zeiss) or measurement of the fluorescence intensity with a Synergy™ 4 multimode microplate spectrophotometer. All experiments were conducted in quadruplicate.

Cells were seeded into 6-well plates at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well and infected with EV-A71 at an MOI of 1 for 1 h followed by washing twice with PBS and treatment with Hsp, Hsd, Hsp-CPDs or Hsd-CPDs for 6 h. Subsequently, the cells were collected and lysed in 200 μL of M-PER containing 1X protease and phosphatase inhibitors for 30 min at 4 °C and then centrifuged at an RCF of 13,000×g for 10 min. The concentration of total protein in the supernatants was determined with a Pierce BCA Protein Assay Kit and equal amounts of protein were separated by SDS–PAGE followed by transfer to polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membranes. The membranes were blocked with 5% BSA in PBS for 1 h at room temperature and then incubated with various antibodies, including primary antibodies against EV-A71 VP1 (1:1,000, MAB979, Millipore), 3D (1:5,000, GTX132355, GeneTex), eIF4G (1:1,000, GTX118109, GeneTex), phosphorylated p38 (1:1,000, #4511, Cell Signaling), p38 (1:1,000, #8690, Cell Signaling) or the internal control β-actin (1:1,000, A1978, Sigma–Aldrich) for 12 h at 4 °C. Thereafter, the membranes were washed three times with PBS containing 0.2% Tween 20 (0.2% PBST) and then incubated with corresponding horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated anti-mouse (1:3000, #7076, Cell Signaling) or anti-rabbit (1:3000, #7074, Cell Signaling) secondary antibody for 1 h at room temperature. After washing with PBST, the membranes were developed, and luminescence was detected with ECL™ Western blotting Detection Reagents (GE Healthcare Bio-Sciences, Piscataway, NJ, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antiviral efficacy of the CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Six-week-old female and male ICR mice were purchased from BioLASCO (Taipei, Taiwan) and raised in the Laboratory Animal Center of National Taiwan Ocean University (Keelung, Taiwan). Permission for the mouse studies was obtained from the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the National Laboratory Animal Center, Taipei, Taiwan (IACUC approval number: 105021). After one week of acclimation, the mice were mated to obtain newborn mice. Seven-day-old neonatal mice were intraperitoneally (i.p.) injected with 10 μL of PBS (mock and EV-A71 controls) or compound (Hsd or Hsd-CPDs) at a dose of 25 mg kg−1 body weight in PBS. After the first 12 h, each mouse was subsequently intraperitoneally infected with 2 × 106 PFU/mouse EV-A71/MP4 and then treated with PBS, Hsd, or Hsd-CPDs every 12 h daily for 7 consecutive days, during which the clinical symptoms, survival rates, and body weights were monitored daily. The clinical grading scale was defined as follows: 0, healthy; 1, slow movement; 2, single hindlimb paralysis; 3, bilateral hindlimb paralysis; and 4, found dead. Body weight was normalized according to the equation below: normalized body weight = Wn/Wo, where Wn and Wo are the body weights of the treated mice on Day n and Day 0, respectively.

The interaction between apocynin/guaiacol and the hydrophobic pocket of EV-A71 was explored by molecular docking using Schrodinger software (Maestro Version 12.9.123). The protein structure was downloaded from the protein database (PDB code: 3VBS). We only retained the protein structure of VP1 (chain A) and removed the other three protein chains, all the water molecules, and the bound ligand (sphingosine). Hydrogen atoms were added using Protein Preparation Wizard. The ligands (apocynin and guaiacol) were downloaded from PubChem database. A three-dimensional space grid box (30 Å × 30 Å × 30 Å) centered at the centroids of Asp112 and Phe131 was constructed for docking of the two ligands to the hydrophobic pocket region of VP1. Ligand diameter midpoint box was set to 20 Å, and the diameter midpoint of each docked ligand is required to remain within the smaller nested box. Glide was used for docking and the VP1 hydrophobic region was acted as a rigid molecule. The binding pose with the best docking score was used for further study.

All experiments were repeated three or four times independently, and the values are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) unless indicated otherwise. Statistical analysis was performed using one-way ANOVA to compare the significant differences among the treated groups and the viral control group. Statistical significance indicated by asterisks in figures is defined as follows: ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.005, ∗∗∗p < 0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CPDs have a polymeric shell and a graphene-like core
ce:section.ce:para: Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs were synthesized from Hsp and Hsd, respectively, by solid-state thermal carbonization at 250 °C for 2 h. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that the CPDs appeared to be uniform-sized particles with dense polymeric characteristics on their surfaces, and the average diameters of Hsp-CPD and Hsd-CPD were determined from 100 counts each to be 98.6 ± 18.4 and 53.4 ± 6.7 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A(a)). By measuring the d-spacing value with high-resolution (HR)-TEM, the value of 0.26 nm for both Hsp-CPD and Hsd-CPD indicates that the carbon core of the CPDs formed as an sp [2] graphene carbon (020 facets) during the carbonization processes (Fig. 1A(b)) [30]. Furthermore, we analyzed the crystalline structure of Hsp and Hsd before and after the controlled pyrolysis process via X-ray diffraction. Hsp and Hsd showed many different, distinct and sharp XRD peaks in the range of 10°–45°, reflecting their high crystal polymorphism through intermolecular π–π stacking (A/B ring of flavonoids) and hydrogen bonding interactions (Fig. 1B). The purified CPDs showed typical broad bands at 2θ = 26.5 corresponding to the (002) planes with an interlayer spacing of 0.33 nm, confirming that the CPDs had a graphitic structure with ultrasmall crystallite sizes in the cores [31]. In addition, the purified Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs showed sharp peaks that resembled Hsp and Hsd, respectively, supporting the presence of pyrolytic Hsp/Hsd polymers on the surfaces of the CPDs. Compared to Hsp-CPDs, the smaller particle size (Fig. 1A(a)), more significant crystallization in the core, and less sharp XRD peaks from Hsd-CPDs are probably due to the glycosides in Hsd being prone to carbonization. Many reports have suggested that saccharide molecules are easily carbonized even at temperatures lower than 150 °C [32-34]. As shown in the Supplementary S1, we observed two characteristic bands, which correspond to D and G bands. For Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs, the D and G bands were at ca. 1315 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1, respectively. The intensity of both G bands were greater than that of D bands, indicating that there were sp2 carbons with some sp3 hybrid carbons in these carbonized polymer dots. Thus, carbonized polymer dots are mainly composed of sp2 graphitic carbons with sp3 carbon defects [35]. Optical spectroscopies were further conducted to characterize the structures of the CPDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of free Hsp/Hsd showed characteristic bands at approximately 265/290 and 380/370 nm, respectively, which were attributed to the A–C benzoyl and B-ring cinnamoyl systems, respectively (Fig. 1C) [36]. Hsp and Hsd and their corresponding CPDs exhibited very similar fluorescence profiles between 400 and 600 nm with excitation at 365 nm. The CPDs possess absorption bands characteristic of their precursors, further supporting that the flavonoid or flavonoid glycoside structures were preserved on the CPDs. However, the broad absorption bands of the CPDs are mainly due to condensation and partial carbonization leading to the formation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (π→π∗ transition) and CO bonds (n→π∗ transition) [37,38].

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was utilized to examine the infrared absorption of Hsp, Hsd, and their CPDs. The spectral profiles of Hsp and Hsd were consistent with those noted in previous reports (Fig. 1D) [39,40], where C–O–C, phenolic C–OH, aromatic CC, CO, aromatic C–H, aliphatic C–H, and O–H stretching were observed at 1040 cm−1, 1200−1360 cm−1, 1500−1580 cm−1, 1635 cm−1, 3040−3120 cm−1, 2890−2960 cm−1, and 3250−3550 cm−1, respectively [39,40]. The spectrum of Hsd was akin to that of its aglycone form, which is different from Hsp. Compared to the spectra of Hsp and Hsd, the CPDs retained most of the characteristic functional group peaks of their parental compounds following dehydration, polymerization and carbonization during the controlled pyrolysis process, suggesting that these functional groups and fragment molecules were preserved on the CPDs supposedly along with their biological activities [38].

Despite the fact that the fragment molecules on the CPDs were preserved, it remained unclear whether these molecular groups were identical. To address this issue, we employed laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (LDI-TOF MS) to identify the molecular fragments present on the CPDs. The MS obtained from Hsp and Hsd indicated a variety of molecules, ranging from m/z ratios of 70.8–326.9, including a protonated hesperetin ion (m/z 303.6) and rutinose (m/z 326.9) (Supplementary Figure 2A). Generally, the fragmentation profiles of both CPDs were similar to those from Hsp and Hsd and consistent with the proposed chemical structures shown in Supplementary Figure 2B, supporting that the functional groups or moieties on Hsp and Hsd were successfully retained during carbonization. Notably, two particular peaks at m/z 134.9 and 165.8, which were abundantly found in the CPDs, represented 2-methoxyphenol (guaiacol) and 4-acetyl-2-methoxyphenol (apocynin), respectively. The former (guaiacol) is a common compound observed in wood smoke generated by the pyrolysis of lignin, and it has been used as an expectorant, antiseptic, local anesthetic, and antioxidant [41-44]. The latter (apocynin) was reported to possess a wide range of bioactivities, including antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer activities and NADPH oxidase inhibition [45-47]. In addition, apocynin significantly ameliorates viral infection-induced inflammation [48]. In short, Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs could be depicted as nanoscale particles containing carbon cores and surface functional groups and/or fragmented molecule heritage from their parental flavanones (Fig. 1E).

Following the characterization of the CPDs derived from the flavonoid or flavonoid glycoside, we proceeded to assess and compare the biocompatibility and bioactivity, specifically anti-EV-A71 activity, of Hsp, Hsd and their CPDs. We first attempted to determine cytotoxicity in a human cell line, rhabdomyosarcoma (RD) cells, via PrestoBlue assays. The cell viability results revealed that Hsp and Hsd induced 40% to 90% cell apoptosis at concentrations ranging from 100 to 500 μg mL−1 (Fig. 2A), which is in accordance with the previous reports [11,49]. Flavonoids (e.g., apigenin, kaempferol, luteolin, naringenin, quercetin, and rutin) have shown significant cytotoxicity toward normal human cells at higher concentrations as a result of elevating the levels of intracellular ROS [50]. The 4′-OH group on the B-ring of flavonoids undergoes oxidative metabolism within cells to form quinones or quinone methides, which directly modulate signaling pathways such as the mitogen-activated protein kinase and phosphoinositol-3-kinase/Akt pathways to induce cell apoptosis [51]. Hsp-CPD and Hsd-CPD synthesized by the controlled pyrolysis procedure showed a substantial reduction in the pre-existing cytotoxicity, particularly Hsd-CPD (Fig. 2A). During pyrolysis, a series of dehydration, cross-linking, polymerization and carbonization processes minimize autoxidation or peroxidase-mediated oxidation to quinones or quinone methides and, therefore, their cytotoxicities. Furthermore, we calculated the median cytotoxicity concentrations (CC50) toward each material according to the cell viability results and summarized these values in Table 1 . Although the CC50 values of Hsp and Hsd were 142.1 and 163.2 μg mL−1, respectively, the controlled pyrolysis carbonization profoundly improved the cytotoxicity, increasing the CC50 values to 267.1 μg mL−1 (Hsp-CPD) and 773.0 μg mL−1 (Hsd-CPD). Due to the relatively high cytotoxicity for Hsp and Hsd at 100 μg mL−1, concentrations below 50.0 μg mL−1 were thereafter selected for the remaining experiments throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Superior antiviral activity of the CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the antiviral activities of Hsp, Hsd, and CPDs against EV-A71 infection. Since the cytopathic effect (CPE) is the most prominent feature following viral infections [52], we examined the morphology of EV-A71-infected RD cells treated with or without tested materials (co-treatment). After 72 h of infection, most infected cells, including Hsp- and Hsd-treated cells, were subjected to virus-induced cell death (Fig. 2C). CPDs significantly rescued the cells at the same concentrations (50 μg mL−1) as the parental flavanones, revealing that the protective effects of Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs were profoundly enhanced. Based on the degree of cell viability rescued by Hsp, Hsd, and CPD co-treatment, we determined the median effective concentration values (EC50 values) and calculated the corresponding selectivity indices (SI; the ratio of CC50/EC50). As shown in Table 1, the EC50 values decreased from over 200 μg mL−1 for both Hsp and Hsd to 44.2 μg mL−1 (Hsp-CPDs) and 17.7 μg mL−1 (Hsd-CPDs), respectively, rendering the SI 6.0 (Hsp-CPDs) and 43.7 (Hsd-CPDs). The acceptance criterion of an SI value for the potential therapeutic should be greater than 10 [53], supporting Hsd-CPDs (SI = 43.7) as a potential therapeutic candidate.

To further elucidate the correlation between antiviral efficacy and the viral life cycle, we compared infected cells treated with the tested materials before (pre-treatment), after (post-treatment), or simultaneously with (co-treatment) EV-A71 infection, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 2B. As shown in Fig. 2D, while both Hsp and Hsd exhibited moderate anti-EV activity, particularly with pre-treatment, the CPD forms of Hsp and Hsd extensively enhanced the antiviral efficacy in all treatments. Notably, all test materials exhibited relatively limited antiviral effects during co-treatment, but higher concentrations (50 μg mL−1) of Hsp-CPD and Hsd-CPD could still rescue the viability of the infected cells to reach 60 to 95% (Fig. 2D), suggesting that CPDs might exert anti-EV activity during the early stages of the viral life cycle, such as viral attachment and viral entry, or potential virucidal effects [54].

To confirm that the tested materials inhibited infectious virions, we conducted a plaque reduction assay to assess the antiviral activities of CPDs under co-treatment conditions. RD cells were infected with EV-A71 at a titer of 100 PFU mL−1 in the absence or presence of tested materials, and then stained with 5% crystal violet to evaluate the plaque formation. As demonstrated in Fig. 2E, Hsp and Hsd treatment reduced the virus plaque formation by approximately 30%, and the two CPDs inhibited nearly 90% of infectious viral propagation, indicating that these flavanone CPDs could effectively suppress the release of viral particles from infected cells. Of note, Hsd-CPDs exhibited superior antiviral activity regardless of co-treatment, pre-treatment or post-treatment.

By utilizing HRTEM, we observed direct binding between EV-A71 virions and Hsd-CPDs (Fig. 3 A), which was confirmed by the observed lattice fringes on the virus particles. Hence, it is reasonable to speculate that the CPDs directly interfered with viral attachment or entry into the host cell. On the other hand, Hsp-CPDs might not bind to the surface of EV-A71 virions as effectively as Hsd-CPDs, as the patterns from the Hsp-CPDs appeared to be far away from the periphery of the virions. Additionally, Hsd-CPDs could have superior binding affinity to EV-A71 virions compared with Hsp-CPDs due to the presence of the glycosidic group on the precursor molecule, as rutinose has been reported to protect cells from the entry of rotavirus in this manner [55]. The viral capsid protein VP1 is a well-known target of antiviral efficacy of naturally phenolic compounds.[56] [58] The specific interaction between Hsd-CPDs and non-enveloped EV-A71 particle imply that the molecule moieties on the surface of Hsd-CPDs might target the VP1 of EV-A71, which contains a hydrophobic pocket entry for receptor binding (binding with the scavenger receptor class B member 2 (SCARB2)) and the most studied epitopes for antibody neutralization [59-61]. In addition, the conformational change of VP1 protein is a crucial factor to lead viral particle disassembly and release of viral RNA into the host cell. [62-64] We attempted to dock the hydrophobic pocket and find binding sites. We perform molecular docking of apocynin or guaiacol to the hydrophobic pocket within VP1 using Schrodinger software because apocynin and guaiacol on Hsd-CPDs play important roles in anti-EV-A71. As shown in Fig. 3B, both apocynin and guaiacol showed binding modes in the hydrophobic pocket region of EV-A71 as predicted. Apocynin has a hydrogen bond with Ile113, π−π stacking interaction with Tyr201 and binding energy of 5.9 kcal mol−1. Guaiacol is also bound to the hydrophobic pocket sites, has two hydrogen bonds with Gly223, Met225, and binding energy of 5.6 kcal mol−1. The pocket entrance in the canyon region of VP1 is known to be critical for SCARB2 interaction to displace the pocket factor such as sphingosine to induce conformational change-dependent viral uncoating [62–64]. The apocynin and guaiacol on Hsd-CPDs bind to residues around the pocket entrance may interfere with the EV-A71/SCARB2 engagement, which suppresses viral attachment and entry and neutralization of viral particle infectivity.

Since the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in mitochondria provides the necessary energy for EV-A71 replication [65], we measured ROS levels in EV-A71-infected cells with fluorescence microscopy with 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assays. As expected, EV-A71 infection induced profound ROS generation. Hsp, Hsp-CPD and Hsd treatments could not effectively suppress EV-A71-induced ROS production obviously, while Hsd-CPD suppressed nearly all (Fig. 3C). From these results, we inferred that the high antioxidant effect of Hsd-CPDs could be due to the formation of apocynin on the CPDs surface during the transformation from Hsd to Hsd-CPDs. Apocynin has been reported to serve as an inhibitor of NADPH oxidase that accounts for ROS generation [66] and is known to inhibit ROS generation in other viral infections (H5N1 and H7N9) [67]. EV-A71 induced the generation of mitochondrial ROS, and a decline in respiratory function is essential to EV-A71 replication. Hsd-CPDs effectively suppressed the ROS generation induced by EV-A71, contributing to its superior antiviral activity.

Due to the prominent effectiveness of Hsd-CPDs, we further pursued more studies on the mechanisms of action of Hsd-CPDs and their inhibition of EV-A71 infection. To this end, we analyzed the expression of essential proteins related to the structure or replication of EV-A71 with or without Hsd-CPD treatment. RD cells were infected with EV-A71 for 1 h to allow the virus to enter the cells, followed by incubation with Hsd-CPDs for 12 h and the collection of cell lysates for Western blotting. The Western blot results showed that the viral structural protein VP1 was significantly suppressed upon Hsd-CPD treatment at 20 and 200 μg mL−1, indicating that the translation/replication of EV-A71 was inhibited by Hsd-CPDs post-treatment (Fig. 3D and Supplementary Figure 3). Moreover, decreased expression of nonstructural proteins, such as viral protease (3CDpro) and RNA polymerase (3Dpol), confirmed that Hsd-CPDs suppressed the translation/replication of EV-A71. Given that EV-A71 infection induces phosphorylation of the p38 MAPK pathway and eukaryotic translation initiation factor 4G (eIF4G), which is responsible for internal ribosome entry site (IRES)-dependent translation after cleavage during EV-A71 replication [68], we monitored the expression of the eIF4G and p38 proteins in the same setting. We found that Hsd-CPDs treatment significantly inhibited EV-A71-induced eIF4G cleavage and phosphorylated p38 expression, confirming that EV-A71 replication was inhibited by Hsd-CPDs. In summary, Hsd-CPDs effectively blocked the attachment of EV-A71 to the host cell membrane and suppressed the generation of ROS induced by EV-A71 by scavenging free radicals (Fig. 5). Furthermore, Hsd-CPDs inhibited the translation of EV-A71 from the sign of eIF4G cleavage of phosphorylated p38 kinase expression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hsd-CPDs is effective against EV-A71 infection in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: After indicating the superior antiviral activity of Hsd-CPDs, we proceeded to validate and compare the anti-EV efficacy of Hsd and Hsd-CPD in an animal model. Seven-day-old ICR mice were intraperitoneally infected with EV-A71 followed by administration of PBS (vehicle), Hsd or Hsd-CPDs daily via intraperitoneal injection. Throughout the 10-day observation period, more than half of the neonatal mice in the vehicle treatment group (>50%) developed symptoms of limb weakness and subsequently single hindlimb or bilateral hindlimb paralysis as early as Day 4 postinfection (dpi) (Fig. 4 A). Other symptoms, including hunched posture, lethargy, ataxia, and even death at approximately dpi 6 (0/10), were observed at the later time points (Fig. 4B and C). While we did not observe profound protection with Hsd treatment (25 mg kg−1) during the experiment, infected mice treated with Hsd-CPDs (25 mg kg−1) exhibited significant anti-EV activity, featuring a high survival rate (10/10) and mild clinical appearances with an average clinical score of less than one, which was significantly lower than those in the other groups (Fig. 4B and C). In fact, most Hsd-CPDs-treated EV-A71-infected mice survived for at least one month (>95%) (data not shown), strongly suggesting prolonged protectivity from Hsd-CPDs against EV-A71. Moreover, immunohistochemical (IHC) staining targeting the VP1 protein in the mouse brain stem and lower-limb muscle tissues showed that fewer viral antigens were detected in Hsd-CPD-treated mice than in the untreated control group and hesperidin-treated group (Fig. 4D). Overall, our in vivo results demonstrate the protective effects of Hsd-CPDs and support the concept that CPDs derived from flavonoid glycosides via controlled pyrolysis could enhance existing antiviral activity without inducing significant adverse effects.

In summary, we have presented a successful approach to synthesize CPDs from flavonoid or flavonoid glycosides using a controlled pyrolysis method. Both CPDs, Hsp-CPDs and Hsd-CPDs, obtained from pure crystals following dehydration, fragmentation, and carbonization, exhibited high biocompatibility and antiviral activity. The carbon core appeared to condense to the nanoscale size and was coated with different functional groups and molecular fragments on the surface. The molecular moiety derived from the parental molecules endowed the CPDs with the original properties and intensified the existing bioactivity. In addition, the CPDs, in particular the Hsd-CPDs, exerted antiviral activity via multiple possible pathways, including blockage of viral attachment and entry, suppression of virus-induced ROS generation, and inhibition of viral replication and translation. Our in vivo experiments further propelled the prospective use of Hsd-CPDs as a potential antiviral nanodrug against EV-A71 infection. While further studies are required to clarify the role of glycosides to inhibit EV-A71 viral infection and the underlying mechanisms of action, our results strongly suggest that flavonoid glycosides, such as Hsd-CPDs, are worthy of further development and warrant investigation in a variety of bioapplications such in tumor and neurodegenerative disease treatments in the future.
